coredata.dc:title: Efficient degradation of typical pharmaceuticals in water using a novel TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst under natural sunlight
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qianxin
Du, Roujia
Tan, Cuiwen
Chen, Ping
Yu, Gang
Deng, Shubo
coredata.dc:description:
Photocatalytic degradation of typical pharmaceuticals in natural sunlight and in actual water is of great significance. In this study, the oxygen or nitrogen linked heptazine-base polymer (ONLH) was successfully incorporated with TiO2 nanoparticles and formed a TiO2/ONLH nanocomposite which was responded to the natural sunlight. Under natural sunlight, the TiO2/ONLH can effectively degrade ten types of pharmaceuticals. In particular, fluoroquinolone containing N-piperazinyl, and cardiovascular drugs containing long aromatic side chains were easily degraded. The half-life of the best degradation performance of propranolol was less than 5 min. The rate constants of propranolol using the TiO2/ONLH were approximately six- and eight-fold higher than those of pristine TiO2 and ONLH, respectively. Two reactive species (
OH and O
2 −) facilitated the rapid degradation of propranolol, which occurred primarily through the hydroxyl radical addition, ring-opening, and ipso substitution reactions. An acute toxicity test using luminescent bacteria indicated that the toxicity of the propranolol reaction solution gradually decreased with lower total organic carbon (TOC). According to the toxicity evaluation of monomer products, the TiO2/ONLH also reduced the generation of toxic transformation products. The effects of actual water/wastewater have further shown the TiO2/ONLH might be applied for the removal of pharmaceuticals in wastewater.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern (a), TEM image (b), FTIR spectra (c) and UV–vis absorption spectra with inset showing the bandgap of the prepared photocatalyst (d) of the ONLH, TiO2, and TiO2/ONLH.

First-order kinetic rate constants for the degradation of ten-types of pharmaceuticals (8 mg/L) including fluoroquinolone antibiotics (enrofloxacin (ENR), ofloxacin (OFL), and ciprofloxacin (CIP)), non-steroid anti-inflammatory drugs (naproxen (NP), and diclofenac (DFC)); cardiovascular drugs (atenolol (ATL), and propranolol (PRL)), and sulfonamides drugs (sulfadiazine (SDZ), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), and sulfamerazine (SMR))) by sunlight-activated 0.4 g/L TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst.

Degradation of propranolol by photolysis, TiO2/ONLH adsorption, and TiO2/ONLH photocatalysis under natural sunlight irradiation (a), first-order kinetic rate constants for propranolol (8 mg/L) degradation by 0.4 g/L TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst containing different TiO2 contents under natural sunlight irradiation at pH 7.0 ± 0.2 (b).

ESR spectra of the DMPO-O
H (1:2:2:1 intensity ratio (a), DMPO-
O2 − (1:1:1:1 intensity ratio) (b) adducts recorded with 0.4 g/L TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst under natural sunlight irradiation at pH 7.0 ± 0.2.
Proposed degradation pathway for propranolol transformation in the TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalytic system.

Evaluation of TOC removal efficiency (brown line, left Y-axis), degradation kinetic of propranolol (8 mg/L, black line, left Y-axis), and acute toxicity test (v. fischeri exposure for 15 min) by 0.4 g/L TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst under natural sunlight irradiation (deep green column, right Y-axis) at pH 7.0 ± 0.2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Evolution of acute toxicity and chronic toxicity in fish, daphnia magna and green algae, and toxicity classification by Very toxic, Toxic, Harmful and Not harmful levels for propranolol and its transformation products (TP-118, 134, 150, 164, 276, 282, 292 and 308). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

First-order kinetic rate constants for propranolol (8 mg/L) degradation by 0.4 g/L TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst under natural sunlight irradiation in the presence of water quality constituents (e.g., [Cu2+] = 5 mg/L, [DOM] = 100 mg C/L; other water-quality constituent concentrations were 10 mg/L) (a), propranolol degradation in actual water/wastewater systems using TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalytic system (b), and four cycles of propranolol degradation by TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst for sunlight irradiation at pH 7.0 ± 0.2 (c).

head.ce:title: Efficient degradation of typical pharmaceuticals in water using a novel TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst under natural sunlight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Photocatalytic degradation of typical pharmaceuticals in natural sunlight and in actual water is of great significance. In this study, the oxygen or nitrogen linked heptazine-base polymer (ONLH) was successfully incorporated with TiO2 nanoparticles and formed a TiO2/ONLH nanocomposite which was responded to the natural sunlight. Under natural sunlight, the TiO2/ONLH can effectively degrade ten types of pharmaceuticals. In particular, fluoroquinolone containing N-piperazinyl, and cardiovascular drugs containing long aromatic side chains were easily degraded. The half-life of the best degradation performance of propranolol was less than 5 min. The rate constants of propranolol using the TiO2/ONLH were approximately six- and eight-fold higher than those of pristine TiO2 and ONLH, respectively. Two reactive species (
OH and O
2 −) facilitated the rapid degradation of propranolol, which occurred primarily through the hydroxyl radical addition, ring-opening, and ipso substitution reactions. An acute toxicity test using luminescent bacteria indicated that the toxicity of the propranolol reaction solution gradually decreased with lower total organic carbon (TOC). According to the toxicity evaluation of monomer products, the TiO2/ONLH also reduced the generation of toxic transformation products. The effects of actual water/wastewater have further shown the TiO2/ONLH might be applied for the removal of pharmaceuticals in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical contaminants are entering the environment which has caused a series of environmental problems. Pharmaceutical contaminants contain non-steroid anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), sulfonamides, fluoroquinolones, cardiovascular drugs, and so on. Among them, propranolol is a common pharmaceutical contaminant that is typically detected in surface water, wastewater, and drinking water (Jiang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020; Fono and Sedlak, 2005). Moreover, propranolol has a specific toxicological significance in the ecological environment (e.g., cytotoxicity, gene expression, enzyme activity, and estrogen activity, etc.) (Kastelova et al., 2003; Takeo et al., 2000; Gabet-Giraud et al., 2010). Generally, the removal efficiency of propranolol through the traditional activated sludge methods remains quite low (Ribeiro et al., 2013; Radjenović et al., 2009).

Many advanced oxidation techniques, such as electrochemical, ozone, Fenton, and catalytic oxidation, have been employed to treat trace amounts of propranolol in freshwater and wastewater (Isarain-Chávez et al., 2010; Chen et al., 2019a, a; Santiago-Morales et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2018a; Benner and Ternes, 2009). In contrast to conventional water treatment technologies, environmentally compatible photocatalytic nanomaterials can directly utilize solar energy without adding external chemical agents or electric energy, which makes these new sunlight-driven catalytic systems an ideal micropollutant treatment technology (Chen et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018a; Chen et al., 2018b; Mahadadalkar et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019b). As reported in many studies, photocatalytic technology has better performance on removing pharmaceuticals in water. Ashutosh Kumar et al. employed the magnetically recyclable terephthalic acid functionalized g-C3N4/TiO2 nano-photocatalyst to degrade the pharmaceuticals during the 240-min visible light irradiation (Kumar et al., 2020). Wen Liu et al. adopted the carbon quantum dots modified porous g-C3N4 under the 60-min visible light irradiation for diclofenac degradation (Liu et al., 2019). As mentioned above, most of studies are carried out under the conditions of laboratory simulated light, and the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants can be further improved. As a photocatalyst, TiO2 is widely used to degrade pollutants (Guo et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020). Recently, Alejandro Cabrera-Reina et al. used the TiO2 to degrade the meropenem in aqueous solutions at pilot plant scale under natural solar radiation (Cabrera-Reina et al., 2019). TiO2 is the most widely utilized catalyst for wastewater treatment due to its photostability, practicability, and non-toxicity (Choi et al., 2018; Gomes et al., 2018; Gribb and Banfield, 1997; Hashimoto et al., 2005). However, as it can only utilize UV light, which accounts for 5% of sunlight, the performance of TiO2 under natural sunlight is greatly limited. Therefore, the development of novel catalysts with high-efficiency photocatalytic performance under natural sunlight is of great significance for removing pharmaceuticals and further improving the pilot photocatalytic performance.

To overcome the disadvantages of TiO2 and other traditional materials such as high preparation costs, poor structural tunability, and low visible-light responses, scientists have synthesized many new organic semiconductor photocatalysts (Xiang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017a), and recently demonstrating that polymeric photocatalysts have a desirable application prospects due to their low cost and controllable structures. In particular, the oxygen or nitrogen linked heptazine polymer (ONLH) comprises a type of soft organic polymer that is similar to g-C3N4 (Wang et al., 2017b), which exhibits a good photocatalytic performance under visible light, and thus has excellent prospects in applications for the remediation of aqueous ecosystems. Our previous study used an ONLH for the degradation of typical pharmaceuticals, and comprehensively compared its photocatalytic activities with a g-C3N4 (Zhang et al., 2018b). It was found that the degradation efficiency of the former was 12 times higher than that of the latter. Similar to g-C3N4 nanomaterials, ONLH is also subject to the disadvantage of low oxidation performance due to the low valence band position.

For these new types of nanocomposites, a traditional semiconductor serves as the core, whereas a soft two-dimensional material acts as a carrier to enhance photocatalytic performance. In this study, a TiO2/ONLH nanocomposite material was firstly prepared. On one hand, the ONLH can better separate electrons from holes in TiO2, and on the other hand, it can broaden the light absorption width of the nanocomposite material, which can absorb wider wavelengths of sunlight than pristine TiO2. Currently, the application of photocatalysis is still in its nascent stages, especially under natural sunlight. Moreover, few studies focus on its degradation kinetics, such as the relationship between the treatment system, free radicals, and contaminant structures. These represent multiple gaps in current knowledge, including the direct use of natural sunlight, the identification of reaction mechanisms, and applications in the treatment of typical organic micro-contaminants.

A TiO2/ONLH nanocomposite was prepared through a simple hydrothermal method in this study and used to degrade ten typical pharmaceuticals. Degradation activities of ten typical pharmaceuticals contaminants (include: NSAIDs, sulfonamides, fluoroquinolones, and cardiovascular drugs) were simultaneously compared. The degradation behavior and mechanism of propranolol, as well as the toxicity evaluation of the reaction solutions and influences on water quality in actual water/wastewater systems, were further investigated in detail.

The details of reagents were described in Text S1. The method of ONLH preparation generally followed the protocol of Wang et al. (2017b). Although several improvements and modifications were implemented in the present study. In a typical synthesis process, the precursor (semicarbazide hydrochloride) was introduced into an alumina crucible with a cover, followed by heating in a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 5 °C /min for 5 h at a constant temperature of 550 °C, and finally, the ONLH sample was obtained. The nano-photocatalytic TiO2/ONLH materials were prepared for the first-time using water-thermal-calcination with different weight ratios (wt%), where the TiO2 content was 100 % (pristine TiO2), 90 %, 70 %, 50 %, 30 %,10 %, and 0 % (pristine ONLH), respectively. The specific details and methods of characterization for photocatalyst were shown in the Text S2, S3 and Fig. S1.

The experiments were conducted under ambient sunlight irradiation (on sunny days). The pharmaceutical contaminants were transferred to quartz tubes, respectively. Magnetic stirring was employed to ensure that the catalyst was dispersed evenly. The adsorption and desorption of the catalyst (0.4 g/L) and the target pollutants (10 mg/L) were balanced by ultrasound within 30 min (Chen et al., 2019c). Subsequently, the quartz tube was removed and covered with tin foil and transferred to the outdoor photocatalytic reaction equipment.

The foil was then removed, with the sample being periodically exposed to sunlight, after which the residual catalysts were filtered out. A water cooling system was used to control the temperature (28 ± 3 °C). The pH of the reaction solution (7.0 ± 0.2) and the optical power density (45 ± 8 mW/cm2) of the sunlight were measured during the experiments, and the concentrations of the remaining drug contaminants in the solution were determined by a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method. The light intensity was recorded with a CEL-NF2000 light power meter (Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology Co., China).

To study the impact of environmental factors, different waters were used to check the degradation of propranolol. The actual tap water, wastewater effluent, ambient river, and lake water were filtered through 0.45 μm filter membranes (Agilent, Japan). The photocatalytic experiments were identical to the ultrapure water process, and each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

For the free radical kinetics study, Dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO) was employed as the trapping agent for hydroxyl radicals (O
H) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −), whereas 4-oxo-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TMPO) was used as the trapping agent of singlet oxygens (1O2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug contaminant analysis and identification of transformation products
ce:section.ce:para: Drug contaminant concentrations were measured by HPLC with photodiode array (PDA) detection (Shimadzu UV model with SPD-M20A; Shimadzu, Japan). The analytical parameters included a BEH-C18 column (250 × 2.1 mm, 1.7 μm); ﬂow rate of 1 mL min−1. A 20-μL aliquot was transferred for analysis, and the detection limit for the pharmaceutical was 0.02−0.05 mg/L. The mobile phase composition and detection wavelengths of the ten drug contaminants are shown in Tab. S1.

To facilitate the analysis of the propranolol transformation products via the natural sunlight-activated TiO2/ONLH, the analytes were concentrated using solid-phase extraction (SPE) technology containing Oasis HLB cartridges (6 mL, 500 mg; Waters Corp.). The details of the method were introduced in the Text S4.

The risk assessment of this study was divided into two parts. The first part was a toxicity test experiment, using a mixed reaction solution of propranolol and transformation products in the photocatalytic process to inhibit luminescent bacteria activity to evaluate changes in toxicity. The second part was a toxicity theory assessment, including the prediction of toxicity for propranolol and each transformation product. The detailed methods were described in the Text S5.

To resolve the shortcomings of the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, our previous studies prepared a novel ONLH polymerization organic photocatalyst, using O rather than N in the CNC bonds (Zhang et al., 2018b). The optical ONLH utilization rate was innovatively improved through atomic regulation, and the recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers was confirmed to be reduced. Therefore, in the present study, the visible light catalyst with excellent electron conductivity was employed to modify the traditional TiO2 materials toward improving the photocatalytic performance.

XRD was used to investigate the crystalline structures and phases of the three photocatalytic materials (ONLH, TiO2, and TiO2/ONLH) to determine the roles of TiO2 and ONLH in the crystallization of the composite materials. Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of the prepared materials, and it can be seen that the TiO2 possessed typical rutile and anatase diffraction peaks. The test results were consistent with the Joint Committee Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS, No. 00-021-1276 for rutile and No. 00-021-1272 for anatase phase). The two characteristic peaks of ONLH at 13.4° and 27.3° were similar to the XRD pattern of g-C3N4; however, the 27.3° diffraction peak shifted slightly to a lower 0.2° than did pristine g-C3N4 (27.1°) (Yan et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2015). According to the observations in previous studies, this phenomenon was caused by oxygen, which altered the layer gaps of ONLH (Zhang et al., 2018b). Furthermore, the composite nanomaterial had the diffraction peak characteristics of two monomer materials, where the crystallization primarily belonged to TiO2, and the diffraction peak of ONLH could be seen at 27.3°. Therefore, XRD measurements confirmed that the TiO2/ONLH possessed two crystal phases, including ONLH and TiO2.

The TEM and SEM images of the prepared composite nanomaterials are shown in Figs. 1b and S2. The surface of ONLH was populated by irregular nanopores caused by the presence of oxygen in the precursor, in conjunction with the corrosion of HCl on the surface of the nanomaterial during heating. When HCl was released, the triazine ring was more likely to be converted to oxygen and N-linked heptazine (C6N7) (Zhang et al., 2018b). Besides, the introduction of oxygen atoms weakened the crystallization of the structure, which was conducive to the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. In the self-assembled TiO2/ONLH, TiO2 was randomly modified on the surface of ONLH, while ONLH served as the carrier material. It can be seen from Fig. 1b that the TiO2 nanoparticles were closely bound to ONLH, without dissociated TiO2 being observed.

The EDS results revealed that the surface elements of the TiO2/ONLH contained C, N, O, Ti and other elements (Fig. S3a–f). The prepared samples were rich in oxygen elements, and the specific components on the surface of TiO2, ONLH, and TiO2/ONLH were further characterized by FTIR. The result was similar to the phenomenon described above, where the composite nanomaterial had two monomer groups. The FTIR spectrum of the TiO2/ONLH (Fig. 1c) shows that the vibration band near 640 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 belonged to the TiOTi and TiO
groups of TiO2. The vibration band near 3300−3600 cm−1 was attributed to the adsorbed molecular water (OH group) on the surface of the material (Yan et al., 2016). The vibration peaks at 1200–1600 cm−1 and 807 cm−1 could be attributed to CN heterocyclic rings, stretching modes, and the respiratory vibrations of aromatic and triazine units, respectively (Jiang et al., 2016). The FTIR results inferred that these additional groups were due to the successful combination of the two monomers. Fig. S4 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the three materials. The specific surface areas of ONLH, TiO2, and TiO2/ONLH were 18.83, 25.84, and 13.68 m2/g, respectively. According to the BET results, the modified nanomaterial had the smallest specific surface area and was generally close to ONLH.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the TiO2/ONLH were further analyzed (Fig. 1d), and it shows that the TiO2/ONLH possessed the mixed absorption spectra characteristics of pristine TiO2 and ONLH. This confirmed that the absorption spectrum of TiO2 could be effectively expanded from 398 to 446 nm after doping with two-dimensional ONLH nanomaterials. Correspondingly, the color of the TiO2 changed from pure white to light brown following ONLH doping.

To study the photogenic carrier transfer, steady-state fluorescence was detected by F97 Pro (Lengguang Tech.). As shown in Fig. S4b, the TiO2/ONLH exhibited an obvious minimum fluorescence emission intensity in comparison with the TiO2 and ONLH. The PL quenching indicated the increased separation efficiency of the photoinduced charge carriers (Zhang et al., 2012). The results showed that the TiO2/ONLH had better photocatalytic performance.

This novel photocatalytic TiO2/ONLH nanocomposite was employed to degrade ten types of drug contaminants from four categories (including: fluoroquinolones drugs, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, cardiovascular drugs, and sulfonamides drugs) to evaluate its photocatalytic activity. As seen in Fig. 2 , the TiO2/ONLH demonstrated excellent performance toward the degradation of all the drug contaminants. The degradation kinetics of typical pharmaceuticals via this TiO2/ONLH system were shown in Fig. S5. Overall, the degradation rate constants of the fluoroquinolone and cardiovascular drugs were faster. Specifically, the rate constants of ENR, OFL, and CIP were (8.4 ± 0.8) × 10−2, (12.8 ± 0.3) × 10−2, and (11.1 ± 0.5) × 10−2 min−1, respectively. The non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and sulfonamide drug degradation rate constants did not vary significantly, where the rate constants of NP, DCF, SDZ, SMX, and SMR were (7.0 ± 0.9) × 10−2, (6.0 ± 0.2) × 10−2, (6.7 ± 0.1) × 10−2, (5.0 ± 0.1) × 10−2, and (5.9 ± 0.2) × 10−2 min-1, respectively.

In contrast, propranolol (PRL, (14.4 ± 0.6) × 10−2) had a much higher rate constant than did ATL ((9.5 ± 0.3) × 10−2) in the cardiovascular drug category. Generally, due to structural differences, the degradation rates of the different types (or groups) of pharmaceuticals were variable, which was related to the relationship between the kinetics of the free radical attacks and the corresponding groups in the drug structures. Nevertheless, the fluoroquinolone drugs that contained N-piperazinyl, and the cardiovascular drugs that possessed long aromatic side chains tended to be more easily degraded. From the perspective of degradation activity, propranolol had the fastest degradation rate by TiO2/ONLH, about 1.13–2.88 times higher than other pharmaceuticals. Therefore, propranolol was used as a model drug contaminant to further investigate the photocatalytic mechanism of TiO2/ONLH.

Fig. 3 a shows the adsorption of propranolol on the TiO2/ONLH. it can be seen that the adsorption equilibrium of propranolol was almost reached within 30 min adsorption. About 16 % of the propranolol (C 0 = 8 mg/L) was adsorbed on the TiO2/ONLH, and the adsorbed amount was calculated to be 3.13 mg/g, consistent with reported results about the adsorption of propranolol on TiO2 (Ye et al., 2013). Photolysis is an important technology for the degradation of drugs in aqueous solution. The researchers investigated the degradation behavior of propranolol under sunlight and obtained the degradation rate constants of ∼0.22 × 10−2 min−1 and ∼0.20 × 10−2 min−1 (De la Cruz et al., 2013; Yamamoto et al., 2009).

In this study, the photolysis rate of (0.18 ± 0.01) × 10−2 min−1 was achieved under direct sunlight irradiation. With the addition of the TiO2/ONLH, the propranolol degradation rate increased significantly to ∼99 %, which was 6.2 and 19.8 times higher than the adsorption and photolysis within 30 min, respectively. The photocatalytic rates of catalysts with different doping levels are shown in Fig. 3b. When the TiO2 contents were 100 % (pristine TiO2), 90 %, 70 %, 50 %, 30 %,10 %, and 0 % (pristine ONLH), the rate constants of propranolol were (2.5 ± 0.1) × 10−2, (3.7 ± 0.2) × 10−2, (5.5 ± 0.2) × 10−2, (7.9 ± 0.3) × 10−2, (14.4 ± 0.5) × 10−2, (13.0 ± 0.5) × 10−2, and (1.8 ± 0.1) × 10−2 min-1, respectively. The results revealed that the 70 % of ONLH doping TiO2/ONLH was optimal. Furthermore, compared with the photocatalytic degradation rate of propranolol in the previous studies, the TiO2/ONLH in this study had a higher photocatalytic activity, 1–3 orders of magnitude higher than others (Table S2) (Santiago-Morales et al., 2013; De la Cruz et al., 2013). The TiO2/ONLH performed well without additional oxidants, and could directly utilize natural sunlight energy in contrast to traditional chemical oxidation technologies, suggesting a promising environmentally compatible technology.

The preliminary photocatalytic tests revealed that propranolol could be effectively degraded by the TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalytic system. According to the tenets of photochemistry, both direct photolysis and indirect reactions involving reactive substances may lead to the degradation of propranolol. Previously, photolysis could be ignored in this system, as the degradation of propranolol via reactive substances was the prominent mechanism. An ESR test was conducted to specifically identify the reactive substances generated by the TiO2/ONLH (Fig. 4 ). It can be seen that the signal peaks were not detectable for the dark control. Under natural sunlight irradiation, the signal ratios of 1:2:2:1 and 1:1:1:1 were recorded, respectively. According to the previous theoretical studies on ESR measurement, these two signal ratios corresponded to DMPO-
OH and DMPO-O
2 − adducts, respectively. Therefore, it was confirmed that both
OH and O
2- were the main factors leading to the rapid degradation of propranolol by the TiO2/ONLH. Moreover, with longer duration, the signal ratios of these two reactive substances accumulated in the system, whereafter the peak response value increased. This also indicated that the reactive substances produced by the TiO2/ONLH were sufficient to degrade propranolol.

The photocatalytic mechanism for TiO2/ONLH could be deduced according to the free radical test results (Eqs. (1)–(7)), where the TiO2/ONLH was activated by natural sunlight (hv) to generate h+ and e− (Eq. (1)). The photogenerated e− reacted with dissolved oxygen through the ONLH carrier, resulting in the formation of
O2- (−0.33 eV, Eq. (2)). Simultaneously, 1O2 was derived from O
2- through two reaction steps (Eqs. (3)–(4)). However, possibly due to the low generation ratio, TMPO- 1O2 adduct wasn’t detected using the ESR technique. In the present system, O
H could be produced in two ways. On one hand, the formation of O
H was similar to that of 1O2, which resulted in the reaction of O
2- and H+ to generate H2O2. Subsequently, the H2O2 reacted with e- to form
OH (Eq. (5)). On the other hand, photogenerated h+ reacted with H2O (+2.27 eV) or OH− (+1.99 eV) to form O
H, due to the strong oxidation valence potential (Eqs. (6)–(7) (Zhang et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. S6, the flat band potentials (equal to conduction band) of the TiO2 and ONLH obtained through the Mott-Schottky plot were −0.50 eV, and −0.62 eV (vs. Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode) corresponding to -0.26 eV and -0.38 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Moreover, the band gap for the TiO2 and ONLH were 3.01 eV and 2.10 eV, respectively. Hence, the valence bands (VB) of the TiO2 and ONLH could be calculated to be 2.75 eV and 1.72 eV, respectively.

According to the ESR results, both
OH and O
2 − had strong test signals in the TiO2/ONLH. Therefore, O
H was likely primarily generated through the direct oxidation of H2O (+2.27 eV) or OH- (+1.99 eV) by h+ (Eqs. (6)–(7)). The charge transfer of TiO2/ONLH may follow the double-transfer mechanism (Fig. S7a) or Z-scheme transfer mode (Fig. S7b). As shown in Fig, S7a, the photogenerated electrons would gather on the CB of TiO2 and the hole would gather on the VB of ONLH, and thus the
OH and O
2 − could not be generated. These results demonstrated that the photocatalytic kinetics of the TiO2/ONLH might be attributed to a Z-scheme mechanism (Fig. S7b). In particular, the e- conduction band of the TiO2 was recombined with the h+ valence band of ONLH, while the valence band of TiO2 and the conduction band of ONLH produced
OH and O
2 −, respectively. Consequently, the reactive oxygen species (e.g.
OH and O
2 −) attacked the active sites of typical pharmaceuticals to initiate their degradation. The above results confirmed that these reactive oxygen species played critical roles in the degradation of typical pharmaceuticals by the TiO2/ONLH.

However, these results differed from many earlier studies, where it was found that visible light responding materials tended to be dominated by a superoxide radical mechanism. In particular, hydroxyl groups were negligibly involved in the reactions of their study. In the present study, the modified materials were innovatively prepared, where the 70 % ONLH doping percent influenced the physical and chemical properties of TiO2, which resulted in excellent valence and conduction band potentials of the catalyst. This property affected the generation of reactive oxygen species in the TiO2/ONLH through a Z-scheme mechanism, which could be further confirmed through the identification of propranolol transformation products in the following section.

To discriminate the role of free radicals in the degradation of propranolol, the trapping experiments were carried out. Typically, benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), NaN3, Na2C2O4, and K2Cr2O7 were used as scavenger agents for O2
−, O
H, 1O2, h+, and e− respectively. As shown in Table S3, with the addition of NaN3 and Na2C2O4, the degradation rates of PRL were decreased, with the inhibition percents of 75.7 % and 65.2 %, respectively. However, the IPA, BQ and K2Cr2O7 led to significant inhibitory effects on the degradation of PRL. These results showed that O2
−, O
H, and 1O2 could be generated during the TiO2/ONLH photocatalytic process. Moreover, O2
−, O
H, and e− played vital roles in the photocatalytic degradation of PRL.

The TiO2/ONLH rapidly degraded propranolol due to a variety of free radicals involved in degradation reaction. However, when the propranolol concentration was detected by HPLC, many other residence time peaks appeared, indicating that it was difficult to synchronize propranolol mineralization. Therefore, propranolol was converted into several transformation products before mineralization. The UPLCQ-TOF-MS technique was employed to analyze the propranolol samples in the TiO2/ONLH system. The transformation product data is shown in Table S4, and it can be seen that eight propranolol transformation products appeared during the degradation process. Most of these remained in front of the parent compound, which inferred stronger polarity.

Based on the MS spectrum, TP-276 was larger than the parent material to yield a +16 change in m/z, which signified an increase in O atoms. The TP-292 and TP-308, larger than the previous compound (TP-276) with a +16 and +32 change in m/z, have shown an increase of 1O and 2O atoms, respectively. Other transformation products were generated via the open-ring and side-chain reactions of the parent compound. A total of three propranolol transformation pathways in the TiO2/ONLH system were deduced, shown in Fig. 5 .

Pathway Ⅰ was the addition reaction. Based on the photocatalytic mechanism of the above results, O
H played a critical role in the system. According to the molecular orbital predictions of propranolol through the Frontier Orbital theory, the reaction sites with higher FED2 HOMO + FED2 LUMO values were more easily attacked by O
H (Ji et al., 2013). As shown in Table S5, 2C, 5C, and 10C had high values (Chen et al., 2019a), and thus they were vulnerable to hydroxyl attack. The 10C site of propranolol was attacked by hydroxyl, resulting in the generation of hydroxy-propranolol products (OH-PRL, TP-276). In a previous study, a biological Fenton-like system was used to explore the propranolol degradation mechanism, and this transformation product also produced (Marco-Urrea et al., 2010). In water,
O2 − reacted easily with [OH-PRL]
+ (TP-276) to form an amphoteric ion or a diradical (Wahab et al., 2009). The detailed mechanism of the
O2 − attack is shown in Step (1) of Fig. 5. However, the amphoteric ions and diradicals were unstable in water, which transferred to the acetaldehyde compound (TP-308). The derivation results of this product were consistent with those in the literature (Anquandah et al., 2013). Subsequently, TP-282 was formed via a molecular reducing cyclocondensation reaction.

Pathway Ⅱ was a ring-opening reaction, with a pathway similar to that of the
O2 − attack mechanism of pathway Ⅰ. The
O2 − attacked the parent compound propranolol, which resulted in the opening of benzene rings. Subsequently, the acetaldehyde compound TP-292 was formed, which was also reported by others (Wilde et al., 2013). TP-292 can be attacked by O
H to produce TP-308 through an addition reaction.

Pathway Ⅲ was an ipso substitution reaction. Based on the attack theory, O
H may attack the 2C atom (ipso aromatic site) of propranolol, namely through the ipso substitution reaction, to generate a stable carbon center free radical (Song et al., 2008). Subsequently, through O-dealkylation, the ether bond was broken and TP-134 was formed. The detailed mechanism is shown in Step (2) of Fig. 5. The presence of TP-134 was also detected during the degradation of propranolol by O3 and photolysis, suggesting that the ipso substitution reaction was an important propranolol degradation pathway (Gao et al., 2018b). As the degradation time was extended, small molecules of straight-chain aromatic hydrocarbons were produced in the system, which was eventually further mineralized to water, carbon dioxide, and inorganic nitrogen. Further, the O
H-induced transformation products (TP-276, TP-308, TP-282, TP-134, TP-164, TP-150, and TP-118) and
O2 − induced transformation products (TP-292 and TP-308) revealed the important roles of these two reactive oxygen species in the TiO2/ONLH, consistent with the ESR test results.

In Fig. 6 , the initial inhibition percent of propranolol was ∼20.0 % (0 min). After 10 min reaction, the inhibition percent decreased since this stage was dominated by the degradation of the parent compound (degradation percent of ∼74 %). At this point, the accumulation of transformation products was insufficient, and the synergistic toxicity was not strong. However, the mineralization percent was only ∼28.0 % after 20 min reaction. The transformation products gradually accumulated and exhibited obvious complex toxicity. At this stage, the inhibition percent was 18.2 % approaching the beginning again, indicating that the produced transformation products synergistically inhibited the activities of the luminescent bacteria.

Even though the inhibition percent was higher than the initial reaction stage (after 10 min), the toxicity was still lower than the parent compound alone, which was consistent with the toxicity prediction results of the products below. However, to effectively reduce the toxicity of the reaction solution, it was necessary to prolong the reaction time. During the middle and later reaction stages, the propranolol parent compound was totally degraded (∼100 %), whereas the TOC decreased from 30.1 % in 25 min to 56.3 % in 90 min, which indicated that the transformation products were continuously degraded and mineralized. Therefore, the combined toxicity of the reaction solution was reduced, and even after 90 min, the inhibition percent was only 7.6 %, less than the initial inhibition percent (20.0 %) of the parent compound. This result also verified that the strategy of prolonging the reaction time effectively reduced the toxicity of drug contaminant compounds.

Furthermore, the toxicity between each of the transformation products and the parent compound was compared. The Ecological Structure Activity Relationships (ECOSAR) model was employed to evaluate both the acute and chronic toxicity of each compound (ECOSAR, 2013). Acute toxicity corresponded to 96-LC50 values in fish, 48-LC50 values in daphnia magna, and 96-EC50 values in green algae, respectively. Chronic toxicity corresponded to ChV values in fish, daphnia magna, and green algae. The toxicity prediction data of neutral propranolol and its transformation products are shown in Tables S6 and S7, and the acute and chronic toxicity data of all the transformation products corresponding to the three organisms were larger than that of the propranolol, indicating that the transformation products had lower toxicity.

According to China's new chemical risk assessment guidelines (HJ/T 154-2004) and EU regulations (No. 67/548/EEC) (The guldelines for the hazard evaluation of new chemical substances, 2004), propranolol and its transformation products may be classified as different toxicity levels (Fig. 7 ). Except for the acute toxicity value in fish, the toxicity data values of propranolol to other organism categories were classified as Toxic (water pattern, Fig. 7). In contrast, except for TP-276 and TP-282, most transformation products were classified as Harmful (light green pattern, Fig. 7) and Not Harmful (green pattern, Fig. 7) level.

Compared with the parent compound (propranolol), the toxicity level of the transformation products was 1–2 orders of magnitude lower. Moreover, it can be seen from the predicted results that the ipso substitution reaction of pathway Ⅲ was the greenest and the most environmentally compatible. Thus, TP-134 was Not Harmful (green pattern, Fig. 7) in terms of acute and chronic toxicity in different organisms. The toxicity levels of the transformation products in pathway Ⅰ (hydroxyl radical addition reaction) and pathway Ⅱ (ring-opening reaction) were equal to, or lower than the parent compound. From the toxicity data above, the use of TiO2/ONLH could not only rapidly degrade propranolol, but also reduce the individual toxicity and the combined toxicity of the reaction solution. This result was related to the free radical attack mechanism. The sufficient O
H concentrations (see ESR test) in the system led to the generation of hydroxylation transformation products, in which this degradation mechanism (via O
H induction) exhibited a greener environmental performance. Therefore, the results further indicated that the TiO2/ONLH was a green environmentally compatible treatment technology with the capacity of being applied for the treatment of drug (e.g., propranolol) contaminated water or wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of actual water quality and TiO2/ONLH reusability
ce:section.ce:para:
The effects of water quality constituents on the degradation of propranolol using the TiO2/ONLH are shown in Fig. 8 a. It can be seen that Na+, SO4 2−, and NO3 − had no significant impact on the degradation of propranolol. In the presence of HCO3 − and Cl−, the rate constants were reduced to 0.124 min-1 and 0.117 min−1, respectively, indicating that both ions slightly inhibited propranolol degradation. This phenomenon could be attributed to the fact that some HCO3- and Cl- competed with propranolol to react with O
H (Chen et al., 2019c), thus reducing the propranolol degradation rate. Dissolved organic matters (DOM) had a significant inhibitory effect on propranolol.

Generally, HA can generate exciting free radicals (e.g., triplet state entities) under natural sunlight irradiation to enhance the degradation of organic contaminants. However, DOM can also reduce the catalytic performance of TiO2/ONLH nanocomposites through light-screening effects in the visible region, thereby reducing the generation of free radicals and further decreasing the propranolol degradation rate. Typical Cu2+ and Fe3+ significantly inhibited the degradation of propranolol by 63.2 % and 39.6 %, respectively. On one hand, metal ions may be adsorbed on the surface of the TiO2/ONLH, thereby reducing the activity of the composite photocatalytic nanomaterial. On the other hand, Cu2+ and Fe3+ could react with
O2 −

= 109 M-1s-1) with a high second-order rate constant, where both ions inhibited the degradation of propranolol by scavenging associated free radicals (Muñoz et al., 2016; Rush and Bielski, 1985; Venediktov and Krasnovskiĭ, 1980).

To further evaluate the applicability of the TiO2/ONLH, the degradation performance of propranolol in actual water was investigated. The actual water quality is shown in Table S8. As shown in Fig. 8b, the degradation rates of propranolol in pure water, tap water, lake water, Pearl River water, and wastewater effluent after 30 min were 98.7 %, 95.5, 88.9 %, 86.1 %, and 90.9 %, respectively. As anticipated, the fastest propranolol degradation was achieved in pure water control in Fig. 8b due to the absence of interferent ions that typically inhibit the activities of photocatalytic systems. Due to the small quantities of NO3 −, TOC, and Cl−, the degradation rate of propranolol in tap water decreased by 3.2 %; where in lake water, Pearl River water, and wastewater effluent, the degradation rate of propranolol was decreased by 9.8 %, 12.6 % and 7.8 %, respectively. This was attributed to the high concentrations of NO3 −, TOC, and Cl− in these three types of waters, which could reduce the degradation rate of propranolol by quenching free radicals, or the photo shielding effect. Overall, the TiO2/ONLH system could effectively degrade propranolol in real waters. Furthermore, propranolol had a low degradation half-life: 4.8 min in pure water, 6.8 min in tap water, 9.1 min in lake water, 10.5 min in Pearl River water, and 8.4 min in wastewater effluent. This confirmed that our novel TiO2/ONLH could be employed to remove some pharmaceuticals from tap water, surface water or wastewater effluent.

Furthermore, the reusability of the TiO2/ONLH for the degradation of propranolol was investigated (Fig. 8c). In four successive cycles, the propranolol degradation efficiencies were relatively stable, and degradation percent was 95.2 % in the fourth cycle. It is obvious that the TiO2/ONLH nanocomposite had high stability and reusability during the degradation process.

A novel TiO2/ONLH nano-photocatalyst was successfully synthesized using a water-thermal calcination method. This preparation method for the TiO2/ONLH enabled the utilization of broad-spectrum sunlight in contrast to a TiO2 monomer. Ten typical pharmaceuticals could be degraded by the TiO2/ONLH under ambient sunlight irradiation. Among the ten typical pharmaceuticals, the propranolol was shown the fastest degradation by the TiO2/ONLH, and the degradation rate was (14.4 ± 0.5) × 10−2 min-1. The EPR results have shown two major reactive oxygen species (
OH and O
2 −) during the degradation process. The degradation of propranolol followed three pathways including hydroxyl radical addition reaction, ring-opening reaction, and ipso substitution reaction. The toxicity of the reaction solution to aquatic organisms tended to decrease with the increase of TOC removal, and the photocatalytic system could reduce the production of toxic transformation products. The TiO2/ONLH was also effective for the treatment of propranolol in tap water, surface water, and wastewater effluent, demonstrating the suitability of this novel system to efficiently remove emerging pharmaceuticals in both drinking water and wastewater.
